title: 1D DP
description: DP problems where the state is a single index into an array -- Climbing Stairs and House Robber, both solved by deciding what dp[i] should mean.
objectives: Define a 1D DP array's meaning (dp[i]) before writing any code
Solve Climbing Stairs (a Fibonacci-shaped recurrence in disguise)
Solve House Robber, where each state has a genuine 'include or exclude' choice

sections.text: Every DP problem starts with one crucial question: what does dp[i] actually MEAN? Get this definition right, and the recurrence (how dp[i] relates to earlier values) usually follows naturally.
You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. How many distinct ways can you reach the top of an n-step staircase?
The number of distinct ways to reach step i, using only 1-step or 2-step moves.

To reach step i, your LAST move was either a 1-step (from step i-1) or a 2-step (from step i-2) -- so dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Recognize this recurrence? It's EXACTLY Fibonacci's shape, from DP Intro, just with different base cases (dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2 instead of fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1).
3. House Robber: A Genuine Include-or-Exclude Choice
Given houses in a row with money amounts, rob houses to maximize total money -- but you CANNOT rob two ADJACENT houses (the alarm triggers). What's the maximum you can steal?
The maximum amount stealable considering only houses 0 through i (inclusive), obeying the no-adjacent-houses rule.

At each house i, there's a genuine CHOICE: SKIP house i (take whatever dp[i-1] already achieved), or ROB house i (take nums[i] PLUS whatever was achievable up through i-2, since i-1 must then be skipped). dp[i] = Math.max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]) picks whichever choice is better.

sections.title: Tracing rob({2, 7, 9, 3, 1})
steps: Return dp[4] = 12 -- achieved by robbing houses at indices 0, 2, and 4 (values 2, 9, 1), none adjacent: 2+9+1 = 12

sections.text: dp[i-2] + nums[i] represents 'rob house i, so house i-1 must be skipped, but everything achievable through i-2 is still fair game' -- this is exactly the no-two-adjacent constraint being enforced by which earlier dp value gets combined with nums[i].

sections.title: Common ingredients across 1D DP problems
points: State: dp[i] means something specific about 'up through position i' -- define this FIRST, in plain words
Recurrence: dp[i] is computed from a small, fixed number of earlier dp values (dp[i-1], dp[i-2], etc.)
Base cases: the smallest indices (dp[0], dp[1]) must be set directly, since the recurrence needs them to already exist

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing the recurrence before clearly defining what dp[i] actually MEANS -- this leads to confused, buggy transitions.
Forgetting House Robber's genuine choice (skip vs rob) and instead just summing everything, ignoring the adjacency constraint entirely.
Off-by-one errors in loop bounds or array sizing -- 1D DP arrays are usually sized n or n+1 depending on whether index 0 or n is meaningful.
Not setting BOTH necessary base cases (dp[0] and dp[1]) before a recurrence that depends on two previous values, like House Robber's does.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the recurrence for Climbing Stairs, and why does it match Fibonacci's shape?
questions.answer: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2], because reaching step i must come from either a 1-step move (from i-1) or a 2-step move (from i-2). This is structurally identical to Fibonacci's recurrence, just with different base case values.

questions.question: What is the recurrence for House Robber, and what choice does it represent at each step?
questions.answer: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]) -- representing the choice to either skip house i (keeping whatever was achievable through i-1) or rob house i (adding nums[i] to whatever was achievable through i-2, since i-1 must then be skipped).

questions.question: What is the first step you should take before writing any 1D DP recurrence?
questions.answer: Clearly define, in plain words, what dp[i] represents -- getting this definition right makes the recurrence (how dp[i] relates to earlier dp values) follow naturally; skipping this step leads to confused, buggy transitions.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: 1D DP problems use a single array indexed by position, where dp[i] represents something meaningful about the problem 'up through i' -- defining this clearly is the essential first step. Climbing Stairs has dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] (reaching step i via a 1-step or 2-step move), structurally identical to Fibonacci. House Robber has dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]), representing a genuine choice at each house: skip it (keep dp[i-1]) or rob it (add nums[i] to dp[i-2], since the adjacent house i-1 must then be skipped). Both need base cases set explicitly before the recurrence loop runs.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: 1D DP: define dp[i] in ONE clear sentence FIRST. Climbing Stairs = Fibonacci wearing a staircase costume. House Robber = at every house, ask 'skip it, or take it and skip the one right before?' -- pick whichever total wins.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What should you define before writing any DP recurrence?
What is Climbing Stairs' recurrence, and why does it resemble Fibonacci?
What choice does House Robber's recurrence represent at each index?
Why does House Robber need two base cases (dp[0] and dp[1]) set explicitly?

sections.title: Min Cost Climbing Stairs
sections.problemStatement: Each step i has a cost[i] to step ON it. You can start at step 0 or 1, and move 1 or 2 steps at a time. Return the minimum cost to reach the top (one step past the last index).

examples.explanation: Start at step 1 (cost 15), then jump 2 steps to the top -- total cost 15.

hints: dp[i] = min cost to REACH step i = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]).
Answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) since you can jump into the 'top' from either of the last two steps.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Tabulate the min cost to reach each step, tracking only the previous two values.

sections.title: House Robber
sections.problemStatement: Given the money in each house along a street, return the max total you can rob without robbing two adjacent houses.
hints: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1] (skip house i), dp[i-2] + nums[i] (rob house i)).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Tabulate max loot up to each house, tracking only the previous two values.

sections.title: House Robber II
sections.problemStatement: Same as House Robber, but the houses are arranged in a CIRCLE (first and last houses are adjacent).

examples.explanation: Can't rob both house 0 (2) and house 2 (2) since they're adjacent in the circle -- best is just house 1 (3).

hints: Since house 0 and house n-1 can't both be robbed, split into two cases: rob from the range [0, n-2] (excluding last), or [1, n-1] (excluding first). Run House Robber's linear solution on each and take the max.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Run the standard House Robber tabulation twice -- once excluding the last house, once excluding the first house -- and return the better result (this correctly handles the circular adjacency).

sections.title: Coin Change
sections.problemStatement: Given coin denominations and a target amount, return the fewest coins needed to make that amount, or -1 if impossible.
hints: Greedy (always take the largest coin) fails for arbitrary denominations -- this needs DP.
dp[amt] = 1 + min(dp[amt - coin]) over all usable coins; dp[0] = 0.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Bottom-up tabulation: dp[amt] holds the fewest coins to make amt, initialized to a large sentinel (amount+1) meaning unreachable, with dp[0] = 0. For each amount, try every coin and take the best.
